summary: RMAC uses a four-stage pipeline (issue discovery → resolution → collaborative discussion → solvability evaluation) with a single model (claude-opus-4-8) and a daily push-forward automation. The system has strong issue-tracking discipline and meeting infrastructure, but lacks model diversity, agent observability, and adaptive scheduling. Several systemic design gaps limit throughput and make failure modes invisible to engineers.
problems: Single-model dependency: all agents (critic, solver, meeting personas, evaluator) use claude-opus-4-8 exclusively — one quota exhaustion event halts the entire pipeline
No fallback or graceful degradation when Vertex AI quota is exhausted; agents silently return empty results rather than queuing or alerting
Meeting discussion rounds run synchronously in a daemon thread; if the push-forward job is interrupted mid-run, partial state is lost and there is no resume mechanism
The solvability evaluator and the critic agent share the same prompt architecture — both are susceptible to the same systematic blind spots in how they interpret proof gaps
Agent logs are only retained in memory during the server session; there is no persistent per-agent run log for post-mortem debugging of failed issue cycles
The issue discovery step has no deduplication guard — the critic can open semantically identical issues across multiple push-forward runs, inflating open-issue counts without real new gaps
highlights: The push-forward automation successfully ran across all 10 benchmark problems, creating meeting rooms and running AI discussions without manual intervention
The issue lifecycle (open → in_progress → resolved) is well-structured and consistently applied, giving a reliable signal of pipeline throughput
Mathematician persona routing (per-field expert selection) is domain-aware and produces more relevant discussion than a generic assistant would
The meeting system persists all messages to disk, making discussion history inspectable after the fact
The SSE streaming frontend gives real-time visibility into agent output, reducing the black-box feeling of long-running AI tasks

suggested_todos.title: Add a secondary model fallback (e.g. claude-sonnet or haiku) for insight and solvability eval when Opus quota is exhausted
Implement issue deduplication in the critic agent: compare new issue titles/content against existing open issues before creating
Add a persistent push-forward run log (append-only JSONL) so engineers can inspect what each agent did, even after a server restart
Add quota monitoring: alert (log warning + badge in UI) when Vertex token usage approaches the daily limit, before it hits 429
Make push-forward resumable: checkpoint which problems have completed so a crash mid-run can continue from where it left off
Evaluate whether one round of meeting discussion per push-forward is producing actionable output — consider measuring discussion quality by checking if meeting messages reference specific proof gaps
Add a critic diversity mode: occasionally prompt the critic to challenge assumptions it normally accepts (e.g. problem statement interpretation, chosen proof strategy class)

mistakes: Over-centralizing on a single Vertex AI model creates a single point of failure — quota exhaustion on claude-opus-4-8 silently breaks every pipeline step simultaneously
Running the push-forward on the dev server (with --reload) previously killed the daemon thread mid-run; long-running jobs must be triggered on the production server
Insight generation was prompting for per-problem analysis instead of system-level meta-improvement, producing misleading strategic recommendations
The daily automation writes state to push_forward_state.json but does not write a per-run transcript, making it impossible to audit what the agent actually did on a given day
Meeting rooms are created unconditionally every push-forward run even if the previous round's discussion was not read or acted upon — accumulating rooms without feedback loop
